title: Unit 02 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Online Identity & Digital Footprints.

choices.text: A person's appearance in a video call only
choices.feedback: Online identity is broader than appearance during a video call.

choices.text: A password used to log into a website
choices.feedback: A password protects access, but it is not the same as online identity.

choices.text: A private message that disappears after being read
choices.feedback: A disappearing message is a type of communication, not a definition of online identity.

choices.text: How a person is represented and perceived in digital spaces
choices.feedback: Correct. Online identity is shaped by how someone presents themselves and how others perceive them online.

questions.prompt: Which example is most likely personal information that should be protected online?

choices.text: A favorite movie genre
choices.feedback: This is generally low-risk preference information, not highly personal identifying information.

choices.text: A home address
choices.feedback: Correct. A home address is personal information that should be protected for safety and privacy.

choices.text: A school mascot
choices.feedback: This is general school-related information, not usually sensitive personal information by itself.

choices.text: A public team name
choices.feedback: A team name is not typically sensitive personal information.

questions.prompt: Posts made online can affect a person's reputation even after time has passed.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Digital footprints can remain searchable and may influence how others view a person later.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Online posts can have lasting effects on reputation, even long after they are shared.

choices.text: A temporary message that disappears immediately
choices.feedback: A digital footprint refers to the trace left by online activity, not just disappearing messages.

choices.text: A tool used to block websites
choices.feedback: Blocking tools manage access, but they are not digital footprints.

choices.text: The record or trail created by actions taken online
choices.feedback: Correct. A digital footprint is the trail of information left behind through online behavior.

choices.text: A device used to store passwords
choices.feedback: Password storage is unrelated to the definition of a digital footprint.

questions.prompt: Which action best supports a positive online presence?

choices.text: Posting quickly without reviewing the message
choices.feedback: Posting without reviewing increases the risk of mistakes or harmful content.

choices.text: Sharing private details to seem more interesting
choices.feedback: Oversharing can create privacy and safety risks.

choices.text: Using multiple fake profiles to control what people think
choices.feedback: Fake profiles are deceptive and do not support responsible profile management.

choices.text: Reviewing content before posting and considering how others may interpret it
choices.feedback: Correct. Thoughtful posting supports a safer and more positive online presence.

questions.prompt: A student wants to post a photo that includes a school ID badge in the background. What is the best choice?

choices.text: Post it because most people will not notice
choices.feedback: Sensitive details in the background can still be noticed and misused.

choices.text: Post it and delete it later if needed
choices.feedback: Deleting later does not guarantee others have not already saved or shared it.

choices.text: Crop or edit the photo before posting
choices.feedback: Correct. Reviewing and editing content before posting helps protect personal information.

choices.text: Ask friends if they think it is funny
choices.feedback: Safety decisions should be based on risk, not only peer opinion.

questions.prompt: If an account is set to private, nothing shared there can ever spread beyond the intended audience.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Privacy settings help, but screenshots, resharing, and other actions can still spread content.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. Privacy settings reduce risk, but they do not guarantee total control over content.

questions.prompt: Which question is most important to ask before posting something online?

choices.text: Does it protect privacy and reflect the image I want others to see?
choices.feedback: Correct. Responsible posting considers both privacy and long-term reputation.

choices.text: Will it get a lot of likes?
choices.feedback: Popularity should not be the main factor in deciding whether to post.

choices.text: Is it longer than my last post?
choices.feedback: Length is not the most important factor in responsible posting.

choices.text: Did someone else already post something similar?
choices.feedback: Other people's choices do not determine whether a post is responsible.
